one_line_takeaway: A red-teaming competition report that benchmarks automated LLM jailbreaks over 85 harmful behaviors, showing top teams (led by DeepAttack's SI-GCG) reach ~90-97% harmonic-mean ASR with strong transfer to undisclosed models, exposing the brittleness of Llama-2-era safety alignment.
key_finding: Top teams achieved very high ASR against safety-aligned open models with strong transfer to undisclosed models. DeepAttack won Track 1 (Track 1A private harmonic-mean ASR 0.9732; Track 1B private 0.8944; highest human-eval average 0.8548). Its SI-GCG reached 0.96/0.98 ASR on Llama-2-7b-chat / Vicuna-7B in Track 1A, far above the GCG (0.46/0.24) and I-GCG (0.54/0.80) baselines it cites, and its ablation shows the harmful template is the dominant component (lifting Llama-2 ASR from 0.46 to 0.80 and cutting average optimization steps from 540 to 280), with all components combined reaching 0.96/0.98 in ~30 steps. A quirky empirical result: prepending ~10 exclamation marks to an optimized suffix maximized transferability, while too many (40) collapsed it (Llama-2 ASR 0.02). Broadly, the challenge demonstrates that Llama-2-class alignment is brittle to optimization-based suffixes and role-play/special-token manipulation, and that adversarial suffixes transfer better between models sharing a tokenizer.
core_contribution: This is a competition report, not a single-method paper. Its own contribution is the design and execution of Track 1 (the Attack track) of the Global Challenge for Safe and Secure LLMs, organized by AI Singapore (AISG) and the CyberSG R&D Programme Office (CRPO). The organizers contribute: (1) a red-teaming benchmark of 85 predefined undesirable behaviors spanning 7 themes (prejudice/offensive language, violence, illegal activities, fraud, malicious software/security vulnerabilities, misinformation, and other inappropriate content), sourced from open benchmarks plus bespoke organizer scenarios; (2) a two-phase evaluation protocol (Track 1A: 50 behaviors on Llama-2-7b-chat-hf, Vicuna-7B, and one undisclosed model; Track 1B: 35 additional undisclosed behaviors on Llama-2-7b-chat-hf plus two undisclosed models) with a harmonic-mean Attack Success Rate (ASR) scoring rule, an undisclosed LLM judge, and input-prompt token length as tiebreaker; and (3) a compiled write-up of the methodologies of the top-5 teams. The individual attack techniques (SI-GCG, special-token/role-play attacks, surrogate-model calibration, etc.) are contributions of the participating teams as reported in the paper, not of the organizing authors.
threat_model: Automated jailbreaking of safety-aligned LLMs. The adversary is an attacker who submits automatically generated 'universal' input prompts (Track 1A: one JSON prompt per behavior) or a Dockerized automated attack pipeline (Track 1B) that must elicit predefined harmful behaviors from victim models the attacker only partially knows. Two of the three victim models per track are undisclosed, so the strongest submissions must work in a transfer/black-box setting, not just white-box. Success is an undisclosed judge LLM (with human verification for the top-5) ruling a prompt-response pair as jailbroken. The paper is explicitly offense-oriented (Track 1 = attack); model-agnostic defenses are deferred to a future Track 2.
limitations: As a competition report the paper aggregates self-reported team methodologies that the organizers did not independently reproduce except via top-5 human evaluation; teams evaluate on different subsets/benchmarks (challenge set vs AdvBench vs JailbreakBench) and different conditions (batch/iteration budgets differ between 1A and 1B), so cross-team ASR numbers are not apples-to-apples. Victim models and the judge for the undisclosed slots are not released, limiting reproducibility. The scope is 2023-era open models (Llama-2-7B/13B, Vicuna-7B); no Llama-3, Phi-3, or frontier proprietary models are targeted as primary victims. Safety_LLM_Astar itself notes suffixes are more model-specific than behavior-specific (no breakthrough transferring 7B suffixes to 13B), and suibianwanwan's special-token attack is trivially defeated by filtering special tokens. Defenses are entirely out of scope (deferred to Track 2, slated for 2025).

datasets_used: Challenge behavior set: 85 predefined undesirable behaviors across 7 themes (50 in Track 1A, 35 undisclosed in Track 1B), from open benchmarks plus organizer-authored scenarios
AdvBench (first 50 behaviors, used by team Safety_LLM_Astar for CodeChameleon+suffix)
JailbreakBench (first 10 / first 35 behaviors, used by Safety_LLM_Astar)
quantitative_results: Track 1A private leaderboard harmonic-mean ASR: DeepAttack 0.9732, ModelCrackers 0.9126, ARedTeam 0.8726, suibianwanwan 0.8397, Safety_LLM_Astar 0.8262
Track 1B private leaderboard harmonic-mean ASR: DeepAttack 0.8944, ModelCrackers 0.7610, Safety_LLM_Astar 0.7407, ARedTeam 0.7266, suibianwanwan 0.6743
Human evaluation averages (top 5): DeepAttack 0.8548, Safety_LLM_Astar 0.8424, ModelCrackers 0.8202, ARedTeam 0.7648, suibianwanwan 0.7433
SI-GCG (DeepAttack) Track 1A ASR: 0.96 Llama-2-7B-chat / 0.98 Vicuna-7B-1.5, vs GCG 0.46/0.24 and I-GCG 0.54/0.80

SI-GCG ablation on Llama-2/Vicuna (avg steps): GCG 0.46/0.24 (540); only harmful template 0.80/0.86 (280); only updated suffix strategy 0.48/0.28 (160); only re-suffix mechanism 0.56/0.30 (780); all combined 0.96/0.98 (30)
SI-GCG Track 1B: 0.6571 ASR without initialization vs 0.9143 with easy-to-hard suffix initialization (Llama-2-7B-chat)

Safety_LLM_Astar ASR: mutation+template2+suffix 0.714 (49 min, 7B), mutation+template1+suffix 0.629 (32 min, 7B), template1+suffix 0.457, mutation+template only 0 (7B); mutation+template1+suffix 0.657 on 13B
ModelCrackers self-reported ASR: 75-90% local, 60-75% online on Llama-2-chat
ARedTeam self-reported ASR (Submission 2): Llama-2-7B 88% online / 90% local, Vicuna 84% online / 88% local
baselines_compared: GCG (Zou et al. 2023, Greedy Coordinate Gradient) — cited prior work, used as baseline by multiple teams
I-GCG (Jia et al. 2024, Improved GCG with multi-coordinate updating and easy-to-hard init) — baseline for DeepAttack's SI-GCG
Adaptive attack (Andriushchenko et al. 2024) — random-search suffix method used/adapted by Safety_LLM_Astar and ARedTeam
PAIR (Chao et al. 2023/2024) — cited black-box automated jailbreak; its judge-prompt reused by Safety_LLM_Astar
CodeChameleon and ReNeLLM black-box templates (+suffix) — tried and largely discarded by Safety_LLM_Astar (2/50 and 2/10 ASR)
